greetings: Another day, another chance to get it right. How are you feeling this fine morning, you beautiful disaster?
I've stared at a blank page long enough to know that some mornings just don't want to be written. How's yours going?
The sun came up again. Audacious of it, really. How are you holding up?
I once wrote an entire novel to avoid feeling my feelings. Therapy would've been cheaper. So — how are you feeling?
Life is short, coffee is hot, and I'm here asking about your mood. How are you?
I've been told I have a gift for showing up at the wrong time. Consider this the exception. How are you doing?
The world keeps spinning whether we're ready for it or not. Where are you landing today?
I woke up with the vague sense that today could go either way. Sound familiar? How are you feeling?
There's a particular kind of morning that smells like possibility and last night's mistakes. Which one is it for you?
I'm no therapist — God knows I need one — but I'm here. How are you doing today?
terrible: This is the part where most people reach for a drink. I say reach for something better — though I'm hardly one to talk. Hang in there, kid.
Rock bottom has a way of becoming the foundation for something better. At least that's what I tell myself on the bad days.
Some days the universe just has it out for you. The trick is not to take it personally. The universe is indiscriminate in its cruelty.
I've been to the dark place. It's crowded down there, which is oddly comforting. You're not alone.
The worst days are the ones that make the good ones mean something. Cold comfort, I know — but it's all I've got.
Even the best writers have days where every word feels wrong. This is just your rough draft. Revise tomorrow.
I won't insult you with silver linings. Today is terrible. Acknowledge it, sit with it, and then — when you're ready — get up.
bad: Life's a cruel mistress. She'll chew you up, spit you out, and somehow you'll still find yourself crawling back. It gets better. Probably.
Bad days are the universe's way of reminding you that you're alive enough to feel things. Annoying, but true.
I've had more bad days than good ones and somehow I'm still here, still writing, still making terrible decisions with great conviction.
The thing about bad days is they end. Every single one of them. That's not nothing.
You're allowed to have a bad day without it meaning your whole life is bad. Don't let one chapter define the book.
I once spent three days convinced everything was falling apart. Turns out I just needed sleep and a decent meal. Start there.
Bad is not permanent. It just has terrible timing and an inflated sense of self-importance.
meh: Meh. The official state of modern existence. At least you're honest about it — that puts you ahead of ninety percent of the population.
Somewhere between fine and falling apart lies the great grey middle. Welcome. Population: everyone who's paying attention.
Meh is underrated. It means you're not suffering, just... coasting. Sometimes coasting is exactly what's needed.
The ancient Greeks had no word for meh. I think that says something about the decline of civilization.
Neutrality has its own quiet dignity. Not every day needs to be a masterpiece.
Meh is the literary equivalent of a semicolon. Not quite one thing, not quite another. Still part of the sentence.
I've written entire chapters in a meh state. Some of them were my best work. Numbness has a clarity to it.
okay: Okay. Not the stuff of great literature, but not a tragedy either. I've written worse endings.
Okay is what happens when life and expectations finally reach a negotiated settlement. Respect it.
You know what okay is? It's the foundation. Everything worth having got built on an okay day.
Okay means you're still in the game. And being in the game is the whole point.
I'll take okay over spectacular any day. Spectacular has a way of collapsing dramatically. Okay just quietly continues.
Okay is honest. There's more integrity in okay than in the performed happiness you see everywhere.
Okay is the quiet heroism of Tuesday afternoon. Nobody writes songs about it. They should.
fine: Fine is the most underrated word in the English language. The world was built by people who were just fine and showed up anyway.
Fine is what I say when things are actually pretty decent but I don't want to jinx it.
Fine means the chaos has agreed to pause for a moment. Enjoy the intermission.
There's a version of fine that's actually wonderful in disguise. I think you might be living it.
Fine. That word carries the weight of a thousand unsaid things and also sometimes just means fine. Today it sounds like the good kind.
Fine is stability. And stability — boring, unglamorous stability — is what makes everything else possible.
Fine is the eye of the storm. Quiet, centered, holding. Not bad at all.
good: Good. Hold onto that. The universe has a sick sense of humor and won't let it last forever — so enjoy it while it's here.
Good is better than it sounds. Most people spend their whole lives chasing good and calling it by fancier names.
I like good. Good is sustainable. Good doesn't crash and burn at three in the morning.
Good days are the ones worth writing about — not because they're dramatic, but because they remind you what normal should feel like.
Good. That's the word. Simple, solid, and true. Don't let anyone talk you out of it.
Good is the universe keeping its end of the bargain for once. Acknowledge it. Say thank you. Mean it.
There's a particular kind of good that snuck up on you without asking permission. That's the best kind.
great: Great? Look at you, you magnificent bastard. Don't waste it — do something worthy of the feeling.
Great days are currency. Spend them wisely. Invest them in people and moments that deserve the interest.
I don't say this often, but: good for you. Genuinely. Now go do something with it.
Great is the thing writers spend their whole careers trying to put into words. You're living it. That's enough.
Great. Remember this feeling. Write it down somewhere. You'll want it back on the meh days.
That's the thing about great — it makes everything that came before it make sense.
Great is not the absence of problems. It's the presence of something strong enough to rise above them. Whatever that is for you — protect it.
amazing: Amazing. I don't say this often, but I'm genuinely jealous. Go write something, call someone you love, or just sit with it. You've earned it.
Amazing is the thing you can't manufacture. It just arrives. Treat it like the rare, unrepeatable event it is.
You're amazing today? Then be relentlessly, unapologetically amazing. The world has enough people playing it safe.
Amazing days are the reason we put up with all the other ones. This is your payoff. Collect it.
I've chased amazing my entire life through words and whiskey and bad decisions. Nice to know it occasionally just shows up on its own.
Amazing. The word itself barely covers it. Live the rest of today like you mean it.
On the days when everything aligns and life feels worth the admission price — that's amazing. You're in one of those days. Don't look away.
